items.title: dataprof
items.subtitle: A small analytical engine hiding inside a data profiler.
items.summary: A Rust/Python profiler shaped around Apache Arrow, RecordBatch flows, Parquet, and machine-readable pipeline output.

mediaSlots.label: Workspace photo
mediaSlots.placeholder: A future photo of the local development setup, profiling traces, or notebook-to-CLI workflow.

mediaSlots.label: Pipeline artifact
mediaSlots.placeholder: A real report screenshot or Arrow batch flow once the next public build is ready.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Profiling data should work like an infrastructure component: runnable in CI, useful from a CLI, and consumable by other systems.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: Arrow batches became the contract between ingestion, profiling, SQL execution, and export instead of a final serialization detail.

sections.heading: What to show next
sections.body: A compact demo dataset, a generated report, and a before/after architecture note would make this page feel complete.

items.title: Ares + Ceres
items.subtitle: Harvesting, scraping, and delta detection as an operational system.
items.summary: A paired architecture for data acquisition work: Ceres focuses on configurable harvesting behavior, while Ares covers scraping pipeline concerns.

mediaSlots.label: Field notes
mediaSlots.placeholder: A future desk or whiteboard photo with collection strategy notes.

mediaSlots.label: Architecture
mediaSlots.placeholder: A real pipeline trace showing fetch, detect, persist, and review stages.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Data collection needs different contracts depending on whether the goal is repeatable harvesting or targeted scraping.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The interesting part is delta detection: choosing what changed, what matters, and how much work to spend finding out.

sections.heading: What to show next
sections.body: A real CLI walkthrough and a strategy matrix would turn the architecture into a usable guide.

items.title: Mosaico
items.subtitle: AI workflow orchestration as composable infrastructure.
items.summary: An open tooling contribution area around AI workflow orchestration, integration boundaries, and developer-facing automation.

mediaSlots.label: Demo surface
mediaSlots.placeholder: A future product screenshot or flow capture from a representative orchestration run.

mediaSlots.label: Contribution note
mediaSlots.placeholder: A short note on the actual upstream contribution and the design tradeoff it touched.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.body: AI workflows become maintainable when orchestration is explicit, observable, and understandable by engineers who inherit it.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The useful tension is between flexible workflow composition and operational constraints that keep systems debuggable.

sections.heading: What to show next
sections.body: A compact flow diagram and a contribution diff story would make this case study much more concrete.

items.title: Zero Grappler
items.subtitle: Cost-aware pipeline thinking for modern analytical systems.
items.summary: A project thread around estimating, understanding, and reducing the operational cost of data and AI-oriented pipelines.

mediaSlots.label: Cost model
mediaSlots.placeholder: A future annotated cost model with concrete pipeline assumptions.

mediaSlots.label: Personal material
mediaSlots.placeholder: A photo or note from the research process once the thread becomes more public.

sections.heading: Why it exists
sections.body: Modern data systems can look elegant while hiding unpredictable cost behavior across storage, compute, and AI usage.

sections.heading: Technical center
sections.body: The core idea is to make cost a first-class design input instead of a post-hoc dashboard.

sections.heading: What to show next
sections.body: A small calculator, a worked example, and a lakehouse decision funnel would make the page interactive.
